output:
Here is a comprehensive biography of **Michael Collins**, the American astronaut best known for his role in the historic Apollo 11 mission.

---

## Michael Collins (1930–2021)

### Early Life

Michael Collins was born on **October 31, 1930**, in **Rome, Italy**, where his father, **James Lawton Collins**, was serving as the U.S. military attaché. He came from a distinguished military family — his father was a U.S. Army officer, his uncle was General J. Lawton Collins (Chief of Staff of the U.S. Army), and his older brother also served in the military.

Growing up, Collins lived in numerous locations due to his father's military postings, including Rome, Oklahoma, New York, Maryland, Ohio, Puerto Rico, Texas, and Virginia. He took his first airplane ride in Puerto Rico aboard a Grumman Widgeon, which sparked his interest in flying. He graduated from **St. Albans School** in Washington, D.C., in 1948 and then attended the **United States Military Academy at West Point**, graduating in **1952** with a Bachelor of Science degree in military science (finishing 185th out of 527 cadets).

### Military Career

Collins chose to join the **United States Air Force** rather than the Army, partly to avoid accusations of nepotism given his family's prominence in the Army. He earned his pilot's wings in 1953 and went on to fly **F-86 Sabre** fighters at Chambley-Bussières Air Base in France. He won first prize in a 1956 gunnery competition and was forced to eject from an F-86 during a NATO exercise that same year after a fire broke out behind the cockpit.

In 1960, Collins was accepted into the **U.S. Air Force Experimental Flight Test Pilot School** at Edwards Air Force Base, California. His classmates included future astronauts **Frank Borman**, **Jim Irwin**, and **Tom Stafford**. He later attended the **Aerospace Research Pilot School (ARPS)**, where training included flights in F-104 Starfighters up to 90,000 feet, experiencing brief periods of weightlessness.

### Personal Life

Collins met **Patricia Mary Finnegan** from Boston, Massachusetts, at an officers' mess in France. She was a graduate of Emmanuel College and worked as a social worker. Collins converted from Episcopalianism to Catholicism before their marriage. They married in **1957** and had three children: actress **Kate Collins** (born 1959), **Ann** (born 1961), and **Michael** (born 1963). Patricia passed away in 2014.

### NASA Career

#### Selection and Training

Collins first applied to NASA's second astronaut group in 1962 but was not accepted. He applied again in 1963 and was selected as part of **NASA's third group of 14 astronauts**. This group was younger and better educated than the previous two, with an average age of 31. Collins was assigned the specialization of **pressure suits and extravehicular activities (EVAs)** — his first choice.

#### Gemini 10 (July 1966)

Collins' first spaceflight was **Gemini 10**, launched on **July 18, 1966**, with Command Pilot **John Young**. The three-day mission achieved several milestones:

- **Rendezvoused and docked** with two separate Agena target vehicles
- Set a new **altitude record of 475 miles (764 km)** above Earth
- Collins performed **two spacewalks (EVAs)** — the first person to do so on a single mission
  - First EVA: A "stand-up" EVA through the hatch to take ultraviolet photographs of the stars
  - Second EVA: He retrieved a micrometeorite collector from an abandoned Agena vehicle using a handheld nitrogen maneuvering gun

The mission splashed down on **July 21, 1966**, completing nearly all major objectives.

#### Apollo 11 (July 1969)

Collins was originally assigned to the Apollo 9 crew but was moved to the **Command Module Pilot (CMP)** position on **Apollo 11** due to crew rotation changes. On **July 16, 1969**, Apollo 11 launched from Kennedy Space Center with crewmates **Neil Armstrong** (Commander) and **Buzz Aldrin** (Lunar Module Pilot).

While Armstrong and Aldrin descended to the lunar surface in the Eagle, Collins remained in orbit aboard the **Command Module Columbia**, orbiting the Moon **thirty times** over approximately 21 hours. During each orbit, he was completely alone on the far side of the Moon, out of radio contact with Earth and his crewmates, for about 48 minutes per orbit. He became:

- The **second person to orbit the Moon alone** (after John Young on Apollo 10)
- One of only **24 people** to have traveled to the Moon

Collins later described his solitary experience orbiting the Moon in his autobiography *Carrying the Fire* (1974), noting the profound solitude and beauty of seeing the Moon up close from his unique vantage point.

Apollo 11 splashed down in the Pacific Ocean on **July 24, 1969**. Along with his crewmates, Collins was awarded the **Presidential Medal of Freedom** in 1969.
